sections.heading: Example adjudicators decisions on congestion charging
sections.content:
Please Note: Just because an adjudicator has ruled one way in a particular case doesn’t mean that he will do so again. Also there are many adjudicators. Each case is dealt with on its own merits.
Case 9050029890 – Resident within charging zone – In this case a resident within the zone purchased a resident’s monthly congestion charge.
It commenced on 26th November and ended on 23rd December. However on the 14th December the resident wanted to check the expiry date so she telephoned the congestion charge operator. She was advised that she was “covered until 4th January” This was due to the fact that the congestion charge doesn’t apply between Christmas and the new year. She entered the zone on the 24th December and received a penalty charge notice. Transport for London rejected her appeal so she went to the adjudicator. No records of the conversation existed but the adjudicator said it was highly probably that the conversation took place and that the driver was told that she was covered until 4th January. He also observed that on the 4th January, as advised by the operator, the driver did purchase a ticket for a month.
The case was allowed.
Sometimes the staff at the congestion charging centre get it wrong.  If you telephoned and were told that you were ok to drive in and then you received a penalty charge notice then you should appeal.
Case 9040232967 – Blue disabled badge holder. 
Like the previous case the appellant had a conversation with an operator at the congestion charging centre. She was told that she did not have to register her new disabled badge as she was already registered. The appellant produced a bill showing that she was on the phone for over 15 minutes. Having been told that she did not need to reregister the appellant drove into the charging zone but received a penalty charge notice.
Sometimes the staff at the congestion charging centre get it wrong.  If you telephoned and were told that you were ok to drive in and then you received a penalty charge notice then you should appeal.
 
Case – 9040035172
In this case a lady rang the call centre on the 9th December as she had to go to London on the 10th for an appointment. However she received a penalty charge notice because the call centre staff recorded the payment as having been for the 9th a day when she did not drive to London. The appeal was allowed and the adjudicator said that “I conclude on the balance of probabilities that the appellant legitimately believed that she had paid for the 10th December 2003 by this method and that the error lay with the call centre operator.
If you have a receipt and are quite clear that you paid the charge then you should appeal. Transport for London should not have allowed this to go so far.
 
Case 9040297907 – Car driven into congestion zone by someone else.
This case was simple. The owner of the vehicle received a penalty charge notice as his car was driven into the zone by someone else whilst he was in Spain. His appeal was refused as the penalty charge is payable by the registered owner of the vehicle regardless of who was driving!
What a country we live in when you can be fined when you didn’t do anything wrong, Next they will be putting points on your licence if someone else speeds in your car!
 
Case 9050075388 – Diverted into the zone due to road works.
In this case the lady, who took a certain route to college every day, such route being just outside the zone, was diverted into the zone due to road works. She followed the diversion sign and another sign said that there would be no charge on the diversion route. However, having been diverted into the zone, she couldn’t see anymore signs, so she took a quick route out of the zone but later received a penalty charge notice.
Her appeal was allowed as the adjudicator said “I am satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the appellant entered the congestion charging zone only on account of an official diversion due to a road closure without any prior intention to do so and against her wishes” The adjudicator also said that the appellant was given a legitimate expectation that she would not have to pay the charge. He allowed the appeal.
It is often the case that, due to road works, that diversions mean a vehicle will have to enter the zone. Transport for London suspends enforcement on the diverted route but if their signs are not clear and a vehicle inadvertently strays into other streets then they should not pursue a penalty charge notice.
 
Case ref – 9050036364
In this case the appellant stated that he had paid the charge by text message but this was apparently not received by Transport for London. The adjudicator said that “I have no reason to believe other than this was a genuine error” However he refused the appeal.
We think this is unfair as this motorist had obviously set up the text payment option and had a right to assume that when paying by text it had gone through. However no confirmatory text was received back from TFL and he should have queried this. Also we would suggest that evidence of the sending of a text be obtained from the network operator.
Next: Contraventions for Congestion Charging

Case 9050029890 – Resident within charging zone – In this case a resident within the zone purchased a resident’s monthly congestion charge.

No records of the conversation existed but the adjudicator said it was highly probably that the conversation took place and that the driver was told that she was covered until 4th January. He also observed that on the 4th January, as advised by the operator, the driver did purchase a ticket for a month.

The case was allowed.

Sometimes the staff at the congestion charging centre get it wrong.  If you telephoned and were told that you were ok to drive in and then you received a penalty charge notice then you should appeal.

Case 9040232967 – Blue disabled badge holder.

Like the previous case the appellant had a conversation with an operator at the congestion charging centre. If you have a receipt and are quite clear that you paid the charge then you should appeal. Transport for London should not have allowed this to go so far.

This case was simple. The owner of the vehicle received a penalty charge notice as his car was driven into the zone by someone else whilst he was in Spain. His appeal was refused as the penalty charge is payable by the registered owner of the vehicle regardless of who was driving!

What a country we live in when you can be fined when you didn’t do anything wrong, Next they will be putting points on your licence if someone else speeds in your car!

Transport for London suspends enforcement on the diverted route but if their signs are not clear and a vehicle inadvertently strays into other streets then they should not pursue a penalty charge notice.

 

Case ref – 9050036364

In this case the appellant stated that he had paid the charge by text message but this was apparently not received by Transport for London. The adjudicator said that “I have no reason to believe other than this was a genuine error” However he refused the appeal.

We think this is unfair as this motorist had obviously set up the text payment option and had a right to assume that when paying by text it had gone through. However no confirmatory text was received back from TFL and he should have queried this. Also we would suggest that evidence of the sending of a text be obtained from the network operator.

Next: Contraventions for Congestion Charging
